data.title: Tesla's Problems Get Bigger And Bigger
data.description:
Article Thesis

Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) (TSX:TSLA:CA) reported its Q2 delivery numbers on Wednesday. Deliveries were down by 13.5%, missing the consensus estimate. The decline was slightly larger than the decline in Q1, and again, deliveries were way below production numbers.

I believe that this indicates that the Model Y refresh does not explain the poor performance, and it shows that Tesla is facing a substantial demand problem, not being able to sell all the vehicles it produces. The combination of declining demand, declining margins, and a very high valuation makes TSLA stock look unattractive, I believe.

Past Coverage

I have written about Tesla here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently when I covered the company's Q1 earnings report in April. With Tesla updating us on how it fared in the second quarter, at least when it comes to deliveries and production levels, it is time to take another look at the company today.

What Happened?

Tesla, Inc. has not yet announced its financial results for Q2 yet, but it has announced how many vehicles it produced and sold during the period, and what those numbers looked like for its energy storage business. Here are the important metrics:

  • Deliveries totaled 384,000, down 14% from one year earlier.
  • Tesla produced 410,000 vehicles during the quarter, with 397,000 of those being 3 and Y models, while only 13,000 were S, X, and Cybertruck vehicles.
  • Tesla deployed 9.6GWh of energy storage products during the quarter.

A 14% deliveries decline is pretty bad all by itself, but we have to take a more in-depth look to see what those numbers actually mean. After all, there can be more than one reason for lower delivery numbers.

What Do These Numbers Mean For Tesla?

It is, for example, possible that deliveries decline due to consumers being less interested in electric vehicles. But it does not look like that is the case -- competitors such as BYD (OTCPK:BYDDY, OTCPK:BYDDF) reported massive growth for the second quarter, so demand for EVs must be very healthy, especially in China, the world's largest EV market. Analysts see the global EV market growing by double digits this year; thus, there is no waning demand for EVs in general. Instead, demand for EVs keeps growing in multiple markets, thus the sales potential for EV companies is good. Overall weakness across the EV market does not explain why Tesla's deliveries are falling at a double-digit rate, both in Q1 and in Q2, with the Q2 decline even being slightly larger than the decline in Q1.

Of course, company-specific items can be at play as well. It is, for example, possible that a company experiences part shortages due to a supplier falling out. In that case, we could expect the company to see its production fall significantly, but if demand is healthy, the company will sell all the vehicles it produces, plus some of the vehicles it had in its inventory from previous quarters. In that scenario, the automobile company would be supply constrained -- due to part shortages or other production issues, it can't produce as many vehicles as the market wants. The same would hold true if a company's production is temporarily disrupted by retooling of production lines, model refreshes, and so on. But looking at the number of vehicles that Tesla produced and the number of vehicles it sold, it is pretty clear that this isn't the case, either. After all, Tesla produced way more vehicles than it managed to sell -- to me, this shows that Tesla is demand-constrained, not supply constrained.

Tesla can produce a lot of vehicles, but it isn't able to find willing buyers for its output. Hence, it produced more than it sold, which was also the case in Q1. In Q1, Tesla had already produced close to 30,000 more vehicles than it was able to sell, and in Q2, Tesla again produced close to 30,000 more vehicles than it was able to sell. Tesla, of course, also had thousands of vehicles in inventory at the beginning of the year -- but that number rose by between 50,000 and 60,000 vehicles in the first half of this year alone.

Earlier this year, many bulls argued that Tesla only experienced a sales decline due to the Model Y refresh. By that logic, the changes across its fabs, etc., have disrupted production, which is why Tesla wasn't able to sell as many vehicles as in previous quarters. But that does not fit at all with what we are seeing both in Q1 and Q2 -- production isn't the problem, demand is the problem. Inventory levels keep rising as more and more finished but unsold vehicles pile up.

The evening following the Q1 report, I was already pretty convinced that Tesla has a demand problem, but the Q2 report makes me even more sure about that. Where did this come from? There are several factors at play.

First, Tesla's model line-up isn't great -- many models are aged (despite refreshes, as those don't really change much about the vehicle), and Tesla's one pretty new model, the Cybertruck, doesn't really fit well with what most consumers want and has a somewhat dubious style.

Second, competition keeps growing, and many peers are getting stronger and stronger. The market for EVs is growing, but that doesn't mean that all companies that sell EVs will grow. Those with more attractive line-ups, better tech, better styles, and, importantly, better prices, will gain market share, while those with worse products and unattractive pricing will lose market share. Tesla faces tough competition in China, such as from BYD and Xiaomi (OTCPK:XIACY), but it also faces mounting competition in Europe, such as from legacy player Volkswagen (OTCPK:VWAGY), which grew its EV deliveries by more than 110% in Q1 (Q2 numbers aren't out yet, but likely showed strong growth as well).

Third, politics likely plays a role: CEO Elon Musk, whatever you think about how, is a controversial figure. It seems likely that he "turned off" some potential customers over the last year, which then hurts Tesla's sales potential. Especially when there are more and more alternatives as more and more companies enter the EV market and release more and more models, it is easy for those who don't like Elon Musk to shop elsewhere.

What Does This Mean For Tesla's Stock?

When a company faces demand problems in its by far the biggest segment -- automobiles in the case of Tesla -- then that's, of course, a negative for the stock. But it does not automatically mean that the company's shares will fall. A strong performance across other segments may offset this to some degree, for example. In Tesla's case, the energy business grew, but only very slightly. In Q2 of 2024, Tesla deployed 9.4 GWh of storage, while that number grew by 2% to 9.6 GWh in Q2 of 2025. A 2% growth rate in a smaller business unit does not offset a 14% decline in a much larger business unit, of course.

Tesla has plans in robotics, automobile driving, and so on, but so far, none of these units generate any meaningful revenue, and they are, of course, not generating any profits, either. The recent launch of "robotaxis" in Austin includes safety personnel on board, and suggests, I believe, that Tesla is way behind Waymo (GOOG, GOOGL).

None of this would be a problem if Tesla were trading at an undemanding valuation -- but at around 170x forward earnings, Tesla is a very expensive stock. NVIDIA (NVDA), which is growing at a gigantic pace, is trading at a mid-30s earnings multiple, for reference. Considering the weak operating performance, the lack of near-term catalysts, the ultra-high valuation, and the lack of shareholder returns, I believe that Tesla stock is quite unattractive today and that it has significant downside potential from where it trades today. This doesn't mean that the stock will necessarily fall, and I am not shorting it, but the risk-reward of owning it is not at all compelling, I think.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Atlantic Union Bankshares: Looking Cheap After The Sandy Spring Deal
data.description:
2025 has been a tumultuous year for America's smaller bank stocks. While the Wall Street players have lifted industry-tracking ETFs to a double-digit total return, regional banks have trailed that performance by some way. Within that group, Virginia-based Atlantic Union Bankshares (NYSE:AUB) has fared particularly poorly. Its stock is still down around 12% so far this year, albeit that represents a big improvement from its tariff-driven lows back in April.

I can't say for sure, but the bank's recent purchase of Sandy Spring Bancorp may have something to do with it. Specifically, I wonder whether the market is adopting a particularly cautious stance towards the bank's meaty commercial real estate ('CRE') exposure.

If so, I would be minded to take on that risk. Atlantic's shares may have rebounded off their April nadir, but that still only leaves them trading for 10.5x consensus 2025 EPS. Thanks to the Sandy deal, those earnings are forecast to grow strongly next year – and that puts the stock at more like 8.5x EPS based on 2026 consensus. That looks cheap next to peers, and it could drive a comfortable double-digit return over the next 12 months.

CRE Concerns To Blame?

Atlantic closed on its purchase of Sandy Spring back in April. Following this deal, the newly enlarged bank will have supplemented its existing mid-Atlantic footprint with exposure to the Washington metro area. It will have over $30 billion in total assets, over $25 billion in loans, and over $30 billion in total customer deposits.

Like I said above, Atlantic’s stock has trailed the regional bank space by some margin this year. Credit fears strike me as one logical reason for this, namely the bank's CRE exposure. To put some numbers on it, Atlantic was sitting on nearly $8 billion in non-owner occupied CRE loans at the end of last quarter, equal to a chunky 43% of its total loan book. Expressed relative to Tier 1 Capital and the bank’s allowance for loan losses (‘ALLL’), its non-owner occupied CRE concentration was around 330%. For reference, the average bank with over $10 billion in assets had roughly 30% of its loans in non-owner occupied CRE last quarter (relative to Tier 1 Capital and ALLL, non-owner occupied CRE concentration was around 195%).

Sandy Spring was even more into CRE loans. These topped 50% of its book just before its acquisition by Atlantic and over 400% relative to Tier 1 Capital plus its ALLL. Plus, with Sandy Spring, you also had the kicker of a heavy geographic concentration in the D.C. metro area.

If that is indeed the reason for Atlantic’s sluggish 2025 stock performance, there are grounds for optimism. For one, the bank has just offloaded $2 billion in CRE loans to help de-lever the post-deal balance sheet. Naturally, that will push its CRE concentration down.

Credit quality trends were also looking fine here. Atlantic’s net charge-offs were a meagre 0.05% of all loans last quarter, down around 8 basis points year-on-year.

Non-performing loans (‘NPL’) and earlier-stage delinquencies also look relatively modest. On the former, NPLs were around 0.41% of Atlantic’s total loans last quarter. While up 11 basis points on the year-ago period, that's hardly a disaster: the average NPL rate for banks with over $10 billion in assets was 0.82% last quarter. As for delinquencies, only 0.23% of Atlantic’s loans were 30–89 days past due. That was about flat year-on-year, and Atlantic was doing better than the industry on this metric as well.

Things also look solid if we go back a bit further. Between 2017 and 2024, for example, Atlantic's net charge-off rate averaged just 0.08%. That was around 13 basis points below the peer group average.

Driving A Discount

Atlantic’s recent financials are a little messy. Last quarter, the bank made $46.9 million in net income for its common shareholders, or $0.52 per share. The latter was down $0.10 on the year-ago period, mapping to a return on tangible common equity of roughly 12%. Similarly, analysts only expect non-GAAP EPS of $0.79 when Atlantic reports Q2 results later in the month.

I wouldn’t read too much into these numbers. Last quarter’s results were impacted by some merger-related expenses, while next quarter’s print won’t be reflective of the true earnings potential of the enlarged bank. Among other factors, cost savings will only start to feed through in the back-half of the year, while Atlantic’s share count will be much higher post-deal. Despite this, Atlantic stock still trades for just 10.5x 2025 consensus earnings per share, with analysts expecting earnings to push on towards the end of the year.

2026 should provide a more accurate reflection of Atlantic's business. This is when the bank will see the benefit from cost synergies arising from the Sandy Spring deal, not to mention the income accretion from fair value marks.

With that, analysts are currently pencilling in $3.82 per share in 2026 EPS. At Atlantic’s current stock price of $32.66, the associated price/earnings multiple would be just 8.5x. That compares favorably to a selection of similarly sized banks.

Compared to the same set, Atlantic's dividend is at a discount as well.

Let's say consensus remains unchanged, and the stock trades for 9.5x consensus EPS a year from now. That alone would drive a double-digit return – and that's not including the contribution from Atlantic's dividend. That's how cheap the stock appears to be.

Conclusion

2025 has so far been a lackluster year for Virginia's Atlantic Union Bankshares, with the stock trailing the wider regional bank space by nearly 15 points. While I can't be 100% sure, the bank's credit concentration may be partly to blame, particularly as it has just bought an even more CRE-centric bank in Sandy Spring Bancorp. Be that as it may, credit quality was looking robust as of the most recent quarter we have date for, while Atlantic's longer-term track record also looks encouraging.

Whatever the reason, the stock's sluggish start to 2025 has left the Atlantic trading for just 8.5x consensus 2026 EPS. That looks cheap next to peers, as does the 4.2% forward dividend yield. More importantly, those figures don't require the bank to move mountains to drive a double-digit total return over the next year. For that reason, I am bullish.

data.title: North American Markets In Euphory Ahead Of July NFP - NA Markets Mid-Week Update
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Markets have officially turned the page on the Israel-Iran conflict, which ended in a ceasefire last Monday. Since then, equity bulls have pushed major indices to fresh all-time highs, with the Nasdaq and S&P 500 both reaching new peaks over the past week.

The Canadian TSX is also benefiting from the improved sentiment, touching a record high of 26,885 just today.

The Dow Jones, which had lagged behind in recent rallies, is now catching up-trading close to 1% away from its own all-time highs.

Adding to the optimism are recent trade deals from the Trump administration, including finalized agreements with Vietnam and the official enactment of the US-UK Trade Deal. US Treasury Deputy Faulkender noted that "plenty of deals" are nearing completion and may be announced in the coming weeks.

North American markets are experiencing lighter trading volumes this week, as many traders take time off during the shortened holiday schedule - following Canada Day on Monday and ahead of US Independence Day on Friday.

In the meantime, markets are preparing for the release of the July Non-Farm Payrolls (NFP) report tomorrow morning.

The data will offer critical insight into the current state of the US labor market, and could further shape expectations around monetary policy heading into the second half of the year.

North American Equity Indices Snapshot

As mentioned in the introduction, all North American equity markets are in a frenzy, with most of them marking fresh record highs this week.

Current Highs in NA Indices (CFDs):

  • S&P 500 - 6,233 ATH
  • Nasdaq - 22,751 ATH
  • Dow Jones - 45,060 (1.15% away, weekly highs 44,705)
  • Canada's TSX - 26,885

US Dollar Mid-Week Performance vs. Majors

The greenback has once again been struggling throughout the beginning of this week against most majors - it is only up against the British pound, in the latest mess-up from the British House of Commons leading to a major selloff in Gilts.

GBP/USD fell by almost 2,000 pips this morning and recovered about 1000 pips since, this trading pair is looking less strong after this move than it was in last week's analysis.

Canadian Dollar Mid-Week Performance vs. Majors

The Loonie is actually recovering quite well in this week's Forex action after having lagged in the past weeks against its counterparts.

CAD is up against all majors except for the AUD and the CHF. Both currencies have been showing a lot of strength as both Swiss and Australian funds are rebalancing their funds out of the US.

Intraday Technical Levels for the USD/CAD

The NA Pair is still maintaining its downward trend and holding in its hourly descending channel mentioned in this week's USDCAD Analysis.

Prices just breached the 1.36 psychological level, and it seems that unless the US dollar appreciates from a NFP report that brings it some strength, technicals seem to indicate that further downside can be expected - bears will have to break through the current support and last swing lows at 1.3540.

Levels to watch for the pair:

Support Levels:

  • Higher Timeframe Key support Zone 1.3560 to 1.36
  • Last Swing Lows: 1.3540

Resistance Levels:

  • Pivot zone 1.3675 to 1.3686 coinciding with the 4H MA 50
  • 1.3740 Pivot turned Resistance

US and Canada Economic Calendar for the Rest of the Week

Except for tomorrow's much anticipated NFP release and high-tier ISM Services PMI data, markets won't be expecting much (as if tomorrow morning's session was not enough!)

For past data, both Canadian and US PMI data points came out relatively weak and all are showing contraction, which translates well with the previous weeks' poor currency performance by both the CAD and the USD.

Safe Trades for the big session ahead!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Alphabet's AI Ambitions: Trouble Ahead For Nvidia
data.description:
What is the most effective killer of future performance?

Well, probably past performance.

In terms of Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) stock, past performance is absolutely impressive.

Of course, this performance says nothing about the potential returns Nvidia can generate from today onwards. However, investors should be aware that every journey comes to an end at some point and that every meter already travelled is behind us.

And yes, I am firmly in the Nvidia bull camp when it comes to the "fundamental valuation of Nvidia shares is quite high" talking points. Yes, Nvidia may be valued at a sporty P/E ratio of 50, and yes, I, too, remember a time when chip stocks could usually be bought for a P/E ratio in the 10 range.

However, concluding that any "xy" valuation means a buy, hold, or sell rating would be making things too easy for myself. It would be as if I were the only investor who had noticed the respective fundamental valuation.

Why Nvidia rules AI for now

No, completely different factors are pulling the levers and moving the stock price here. There is the revenue growth from $16.6 billion in 2021 to $130.5 billion in FY 2025. During the same period, EPS rose from $0.17 to $2.94. And if we zoom in further, we see how closely Nvidia is intertwined with the megatrend of AI, for example.

Companies currently have no choice but to use Nvidia for training large foundation models. As a result, millions of models, frameworks, and entire research pipelines are optimized for Nvidia. This means that anyone who wants to use other hardware may have to use new code in some cases.

More than 4 million global developers rely on Nvidia's CUDA software platform to build AI and other apps.

What matters is the fact that people have been using CUDA for 15 years, and they have built code around it. (Source: Reuters.)

Nvidia does not just sell (interchangeable) hardware but has built a platform-based ecosystem with very high switching costs (acknowledging that there are no major recurring revenues, see also below). As a result, data center revenue grew to $39.1 billion in Q1 2026, representing 73% year-on-year growth and 10% quarter-on-quarter growth.

This is impressive growth, despite the fact that export restrictions prevented the company from generating around $2.5 billion in revenue with H20 chips. With revenue of $0.57 billion in the automotive segment, $3.8 billion in the gaming segment, and $0.51 billion in the professional visualization segment, Nvidia is more or less a pure AI giant and the symbolic figure of the shovel seller in the gold rush.

Nvidia itself has not yet issued a forecast for FY 2026, but analysts expect Nvidia to increase EPS by 43% in FY 2026 and by 34% in FY 2027 (which ends on January 1, 2027).

The forward P/E for the end of FY 2027 would therefore be just 26.6.

So let's revert to the beginning...

So what significance does the supposedly high P/E ratio have today?

Given Nvidia’s current strong market position and expected earnings growth, I don’t consider it significant.

I would even go so far as to say that the P/E ratio of 50 theoretically (!) still has room for improvement.

But this brings us to the really crucial point. The decisive factor is how well Nvidia can maintain its current market position.

And here, Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL) is a serious risk that, together with other aspects, justifies a sell rating.

Will Alphabet eat Nvidia’s lunch?

You've probably heard the news.

At the end of last month, it was announced that OpenAI would lease Google Cloud TPUs (Tensor Processing Units) for the first time, even though it continues to rely primarily on Nvidia GPUs and CoreWeave (CRWV). The TPUs will mainly be used for inference of OpenAI models.

Google Cloud TPU is essentially a cloud platform that allows companies (such as OpenAI) to access Alphabet’s own ASICs. In theory, Google Cloud TPUs can be used to train models and perform inferences.

Alphabet has a few key advantages here, which (and I may be exaggerating somewhat when it comes to the wording), I believe, are essentially the next stage of evolution.

Google Cloud TPU provides Alphabet customers with a fully functional environment. Customers can book capacity within seconds. Furthermore, Alphabet takes care of patches and security. All this makes Google Cloud TPU essentially a one-stop solution and, therefore, very attractive for customers such as OpenAI.

So while Nvidia-based environments are in the hands of large clouds and on-premise customers, Google delivers an extremely seamless end-to-end experience. This can be a decisive factor for fast, scalable, and low-maintenance operation of large inference workloads, especially when time and engineering resources are scarce. And inference is likely to be the decisive factor in the future, precisely because models such as DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) show that foundation models can now be trained relatively easily and cost-effectively. Another advantage for Alphabet is the widespread distribution of Google-operated data centers. According to the official location list, AI Hypercomputer is available in dozens of zones and regions across five continents. Nvidia, on the other hand, is dependent on where its respective cloud partners have set up so-called Nvidia clusters, see for example the announcement by Oracle (ORCL) here (emphasis added):

“Oracle’s integration of Nvidia AI Enterprise on the OCI Console will deliver a seamless experience to speed the development and deployment of generative, agentic, and physical AI across local regions.”

Google's one cloud solution also has an advantage when it comes to cross-regional scaling. Nvidia’s model requires classic multi-cloud management, which not only increases monitoring costs but also negatively impacts latency.

In short, in addition to Advanced Micro Devices' (AMD) MI300 series for AI training workloads and Broadcom (AVGO) chips in the inference area, I see Alphabet as one of the biggest threats to Nvidia in inference.

Admittedly, TPUs are not a major risk for Nvidia at present.

According to estimates, Alphabet had TPU shipments of between $6 billion and $9 billion in 2024. Nvidia’s data center revenue was $115.3 billion in FY 2025. Alphabet, therefore, sells only a fraction of what Nvidia sells. According to other media reports, TPU’s market share could be between 5% and 6% in 2025, giving us a fairly consistent picture of the balance of power.

Conversely, OpenAI’s move shows that the company is at least partly moving away from a single-vendor solution. This may be for strategic reasons, such as to reduce dependencies, but it may also be performance-driven, for example, to test the capabilities of Google TPUs.

In addition, the last few months have highlighted how fast-moving the AI market is. I therefore see Google TPUs not only as another argument in favor of my Alphabet bull case but also as a clear risk flag regarding Nvidia’s market position. The market for training foundation models will eventually become saturated, either because training models will become cheaper or, more importantly, because the major consumers of computing capacity, which have primarily been hyperscalers, will switch to their own AI chips.

What does all that mean for Nvidia shareholders?

In Q1 2026, just four customers accounted for 54% of total revenue. These are likely to be Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet, and Meta (META). Given the developments outlined above regarding TPUs, increasingly lower training costs, etc., I do not see any moat around Nvidia's business model, especially when it comes to inference. Besides Alphabet, Amazon and Microsoft in particular are pushing ahead with ambitious initiatives to reduce their dependence on Nvidia’s GPUs.

In addition, it is now also obvious that the growth momentum is cooling significantly. Nvidia expects revenue of $45 billion for Q2 2026. In Q2 2025, revenue was $30 billion, which would mean revenue growth of "only" 50%. Even if we add the $8 billion in unrealized revenue due to export controls forecast by Nvidia, growth would be 76%. Still impressive, no question, but that would also be the lowest growth since Q2 2024.

I expect a sustained trend reversal towards a continuously declining growth momentum, which could probably push revenue growth down to the 15% to 20% range in the coming years. Just to remind you, the majority of Nvidia’s revenue still comes from hardware sales in the form of traditional one-off sales. This is another difference to Google TPUs, which are part of Google Cloud and are subject to a subscription or pay-as-you-go model (which is much more attractive to customers as there are no high upfront payments). Such growth expectation is also in line with what analysts expect for the years following FY 2027.

The gross profit margin also declined sharply to 60.5% in Q1 2026, resulting in EPS of $0.76, significantly lower than the $0.89 in Q4 2025. Yes, I know, Nvidia was affected by a $4.5 billion write-down on H20 chips. Added to this is $2.5 billion in lost revenue due to export controls. So we are talking about (hopefully) one-time costs. Nevertheless, the margin would otherwise have been 71.3%, which would correspond to EPS of $0.96. But even 71.3% would still have been the lowest margin since Q2, 2024.

And who is to say that the geopolitical headwinds in the coming quarters will not have similar effects, leading to more "one-time effects," and when will investors stop glossing over the changing narrative with "one-time costs"? This brings me to my conclusion. I expect the narrative surrounding Nvidia to shift in the coming quarters. OpenAI will only be the beginning, and my prediction is that we will see other major AI players increasingly turning to alternative sources. At the same time, hyperscalers will also increasingly rely on their own chips. Nvidia will therefore have no choice but to lower the prices of its own hardware, which is also a margin killer given rising R&D costs (up 46% in Q1 2026 YoY alone).

The question is how investors will price a stock that is exposed to such competition and will no longer come close to past growth figures, but will “only” have a CAGR of, say, 10% to 20%. At the upper end of this range, I personally consider a profit multiple of perhaps 28 to 30 to be fair, acknowledging that this is already a generous assessment for a company without recurring revenue and without all the associated effects such revenues have on the bottom line. So, considering such multiples fair, Nvidia will be where in one or two years, based on the current share price and expected earnings. In this respect, the share price already factors in a lot of future growth, which brings me to my first sentence: past performance kills future performance.

Conclusion

Nvidia is undoubtedly an outstanding company. Its extremely strong market position in the AI sector, impressive revenue growth in recent years, and powerful ecosystem with high switching costs (keyword: CUDA platform) clearly speak in favor of Nvidia as a company. Nvidia has clearly achieved a kind of monopoly position, particularly in the training of large foundation models, which seems difficult to challenge in the short term.

Nevertheless, I expect the narrative surrounding Nvidia’s growth story to change in the coming quarters. The competitive landscape in AI, especially in the area of inference, is changing rapidly. This is precisely where I see the weak point in Nvidia’s growth story. The fact that OpenAI is now testing capacities at Google is an early warning sign and could be just the beginning of a more comprehensive diversification. Added to this are geopolitical uncertainties, export restrictions, and Nvidia’s high dependence on a few large customers, especially hyperscalers such as Amazon, Microsoft, you name it. Therefore, I expect revenue growth to decline in the medium term, accompanied by increasing margin pressure.

When I weigh all these factors against each other, I conclude that investors would be well advised to slowly withdraw capital from Nvidia and look for more attractive alternatives. In my view, Alphabet is currently an excellent choice.

Of course, there will be voices pointing to Nvidia’s strong past performance. Investors who currently hold Nvidia in their portfolios could certainly let their gains run, but they should definitely be prepared to pull the plug as soon as further clear signs of intensifying competition and margin deterioration emerge. Overall, I therefore consider my Sell rating to be justified.

data.title: Sony Continues To Be Cheap Relative To Peers
data.description:
I initiated coverage on Sony Group Corporation (NYSE:SONY) just more than 3 years ago with a thesis that the company's sum of the parts was worth much more than what it was back then. While the stock has returned 13% compounded since then, that trailed the S&P 500's 17% return over the same period. Most of the stock's returns, however, came in the past 12 months, with it sitting flat for the previous two years. It is also worth noting that the stock has hit my initial target of a split-adjusted $25 a share, though that had been trimmed to $21 in the most recent article.

I still believe there is more upside in the stock, and this article will update the breakdown of where the stock should be trading on a sum-of-the-parts' basis.

Gaming

I use Nintendo Co., Ltd. (OTCPK:NTDOY) and Electronic Arts (EA) as my comps for Sony's gaming division. There is a case to be made that Nintendo is the perfect fit for Sony's gaming division, but as we shall see, having another company in the form of EA (one of the world's biggest video game companies) helps keep the pricing somewhat more sensible.

Here is a look at each company's operating income and multiple on the day of announcing the result. Sony Gaming's multiple is the average of the other two companies.

Sony has both the biggest and fastest-growing operating income of the three companies, with its operating income growing 35.4% YoY. In fact, Sony's gaming unit was the only one among the 3 to grow operating income during the period. Yet, I assigned it a lower multiple than Nintendo.

Assigning the average multiple of 37.7 to Sony gaming prices the unit at 102.5 billion. This is 2.5x the valuation of last year, primarily due to the growth in Sony's operating income and the astounding increase in Nintendo's multiple.

Music

Sony's music unit has straightforward comps in Universal Music Group N.V. (OTCPK:UMGNF) and Warner Music Group Corp. (WMG). Sony's music unit was the company's biggest based on operating income last year.

I am switching to a market cap to EBITDA comp. this year. This was driven by 2 factors: 1) It is quite difficult to get an official operating income figure for UMG. That's because they rely more on EBITDA and don't publish financial statements, which would mean I'd have to make an educated guess of the March quarter's operating income. 2) Sony does not break out debt by segment, which makes assigning an EV/EBITDA multiple to it impossible.

I preferred to provide the audited and official figures to readers rather than my own estimates, even if the price/EBITDA multiple has limitations and isn't the most conventional.

Universal Music Group: EBITDA $3.2 billion, Price/EBITDA: 15x

Warner Music Group: EBITDA $1.27 billion, Price/EBITDA: 11.36x

Sony Music: EBITDA $2.9 billion, Avg. Price/EBITDA: 13.2x

Based on the average multiple of both UMG and WMG, Sony Music would be priced at just above $38 billion. That is 15.5% lower than in the previous article. It is also the second year running that the unit sees declining valuation, and again it was due to contracting multiple of its peers. This is despite strong EBITDA growth of 15% for Sony's music unit YoY.

Pictures

Sony Pictures' initial comp was Warner Bros. Discovery. That was changed however, to Lionsgate Studios Corp. (LION) once the company split its studio business. One of the key reasons for the change back then was that the cable segment of WBD didn't make the comp ideal for Sony. Next year will probably see another modification, with WBD added to the comp once they split their company.

Sony Pictures' performance was again challenged, with both revenue and operating income remaining flat YoY in Yen, and down 5.6% in USD terms.

Lionsgate, meanwhile, saw its revenue grow 7%. It reported operating income of $227.2 million, down 16.6%. The operating income I cited adds back restructuring costs for both years. I follow Graham's thinking of excluding restructuring costs for any one year but including them over multi-year periods, in a bid to determine the true earning power of a business. Excluding restructuring costs also provides better comparability to how Sony presents its pictures segment.

Lionsgate is selling for $1.75 billion, or 7.7x operating income. Applying that multiple to Sony's operating income of $759 million prices the unit at $5.8 billion. That is down slightly from last year's $6 billion valuation, driven by Lionsgate's declining operating income.

There could be more developments regarding the unit, and I will discuss that towards the end of the article.

Consumer Electronics & Imaging

Operating income for the unit came in at $2.9 billion. That is around 11.5% growth compared to the previous year. Revenue growth was particularly robust in the imaging unit, with growth almost reaching 14%. That came with improved margins, and the imaging unit's operating income was up more than 27%.

Samsung, Sony's comp, trades at roughly 10.7x operating income. Applying that multiple to Sony's unit prices, it is at $31 billion. That is 21% below its pricing of last year, mostly due to a lower multiple.

Samsung typically sees wild swings in its multiples, due to the volatility in its operating income associated with the memory business. That is a line of business that Sony doesn't have and makes the comp. a bit harder, though the rest of the businesses overlap quite nicely. I continue to evaluate potentially better comps and wouldn't hesitate to make adjustments in the future if I find a better fit.

Despite the Stock's Rise, Sony Still Trades at Discount to Peers

Adding all the units together implies that Sony should be trading at a market cap. of $177.3 billion, or $29 a share. It currently trades at almost $25 a share, a 13.8% discount.

It is worth noting that all business units saw their multiples get compressed this year. The exception was gaming, which expanded significantly, due to the huge expansion in Nintendo's multiple.

That is something to keep an eye on, as if Nintendo was trading at a more reasonable multiple of 25x, Sony's stock would be fairly valued.

For now, the company continues to grow revenues and improve margins. The financial unit's spin-off is also yet to come, and there could be more developments in the future that create shareholder value, so I'm keeping my buy rating on the stock.

Update on the Spinoff

The spinoff of the financial unit is on track to be completed in October. As discussed in my previous article, this spin-off will be taxable to those holding the American Depositary shares, but not those owning the Japanese shares. It is still unclear whether that reality will lead to some volatility ahead of the spin. I did expect the spin-off to lead to a multiple expansion, and the stock has seen a nice run ahead of the spin-off.

Could Sony Spin its Pictures Segment Next?

The table above shows exactly why Sony management could consider spinning off its pictures segment. The unit's returns are far, far below any of its other segments.

Now, that alone does not mean Sony will spin off its pictures unit. However, the company is in the midst of spinning off its financial unit. And as a result, it is not unreasonable to see management decide to spin off that unit in the future.

There is also a lot of consolidation in the space, and Sony could go the route of WBD and Lionsgate and separate out its studio. We also know that Sony, along with Apollo, did make an offer for Paramount a little over a year ago. It is possible that management is indeed looking at ways to improve that business, and has demonstrated so with the Paramount bid, and so spinning out pictures is not out of the realm of possibility.

Investors should not view this as speculation, but rather as the potential the company still has in terms of further shareholder value creation.

Why Sony Probably Won't Spin Out its Pictures Segment

Management was very clear that they remain committed to their pivot towards being an entertainment company. So much so that investors are better off thinking of future improvement in the segment's ROIC, with a spin-off ultimately just a nice optionality embedded in the stock.

Here is CEO Hiroki Totoki from the latest earnings call:

I'd like to first take a mid to long-term look at Sony Group's business and our overall strategic direction. Over the last several years, our business direction has shifted significantly towards entertainment. The decision to lean more heavily into this space was driven, of course, by the strength and growth of our entertainment businesses.

But it was also driven by the power of entertainment to move people and to fill the world with emotion, which is, of course, at the heart of Sony Group's purpose. In recent years, we have leaned heavily into expanding content in each category, such as games, music, film, and TV programs. IP expansion across our various businesses, strategic investments in content, music catalogs, and growth areas such as Anime, and the development and use of innovative technologies to support creation, all of which have been transformative for the Sony Group.

This doesn't sound like the language of a CEO considering spinning off the pictures segment. The earnings call also makes it clear that management believes there is a flywheel from having all units under one roof, which allows IP to be monetized multiple ways. Totoki adds:

We see SPE (Sony Pictures Entertainment) as a central hub for many of the synergistic cross-company collaborations that are central to our creative entertainment vision. In recent years, we have seen the value and potential of well-executed cross-business collaborations across the Sony Group companies. Examples include PlayStation productions, adaptation of our outstanding game IP, such as Uncharted and The Last of Us, to film and television, with over 10 titles currently in production. Or the many collaborations among Sony Music artists and our other businesses, and among our Sony Technologies and many of our creative businesses. As we look ahead, we will lean into broader collaborations in Anime and through our engagement platform initiative, we will aim to create opportunities and synergies by connecting fan communities within and outside Sony across various entertainment sectors and in our location-based entertainment efforts.

Thus, it seems reasonable in the medium term to look at the entertainment businesses as one unit, then assess the options available if returns are disappointing. So far, over the past year in particular, the stock has done well, and so expectations of changes to the structure should be curbed for now.

Sony Remains A Buy

Despite Sony reaching my original target of $25 a share for a compounded return of 13% during that coverage period (4% below the S&P 500), the valuation of the company's peers has grown. I believe it is still worth buying today with an implied price target of $29 if it's to trade on par with peers, and that's excluding its financials unit.

One of the big risks I have mentioned ever since the start of the coverage was the potential compression in the valuation of peers. It could be more evident than in this year, with Nintendo trading at 49.5x operating income. Nintendo's multiple is single-handedly responsible for the increase in Sony's price target, as the valuation of all other peers has actually compressed during the period. So investors must factor that risk into their investment decision.

In the long term, Sony seems to be developing a flywheel by keeping all its entertainment businesses together. Management seems focused on keeping the PlayStation momentum going while improving the profitability of the music business.

Such improvements should eventually see the stock's value increase over time, with potential for spin-offs of its pictures segment if the current strategy disappoints.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

4-Factor Dividend Growth Portfolio

My 4-Factor Dividend Growth Strategy is an alternative investment strategy to the popular Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD). You can think of it as having your own custom version of SCHD, tailored to your personal investing style. Let me tell you how it works and the results it has attained thus far.

The 4-factor dividend growth strategy was launched on November 1st, 2022. You can read about the strategy, stock selection process, and portfolio construction in this 4-Factor Dividend Growth Portfolio article. In a nutshell, the strategy leverages the stock selection process of Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF, or rather its underlying index, the Dow Jones US Dividend 100 Index (DJUSDIV), with a few minor modifications.

Since I prefer a more growth-oriented investment style relative to what SCHD's underlying index offers, I built my own custom investable universe to which I apply a slightly modified stock selection process. This is a fully automated process with no manual decision-making on my part.

This portfolio is rebalanced with newly identified stocks on November 1st of each year. You can read more about the most recent rebalancing in this article.

The portfolio shown above is the original "pilot portfolio" I dedicated to this strategy. No further capital has been allocated to this portfolio since inception, primarily to simplify tracking the results. I have allocated significantly more capital to this strategy in another brokerage account, but don't share the combined results to keep this analysis as simple as possible.

June 2025 Results

The portfolio added +2.77% during the month of June, while the S&P 500 Total Return Index gained +5.09%. SCHD performed worse than SPY and my portfolio last month, with a gain of +2.24%.

8 months into FY3, the return for my portfolio stands at +0.49%, compared to +9.76% for SPY and -3.48% for SCHD.

Since its inception in November 2022, the portfolio has a CAGR of 15.99%, compared to 21.16% for the S&P 500 and 6.91% for SCHD.

Beating the S&P 500 or SCHD is not a primary objective, but it is useful to see how the portfolio measures up against these alternative investment options. The goal is for this portfolio to deliver a long-term (5+ years) CAGR of at least 12%. Even though FY3 is not delivering as good a return as the prior year, the long-term CAGR is holding steady, well above my target.

The average gain for all 20 holdings in June was 2.98%. This return differs from the portfolio return due to portfolio drift and the compounding effect, since this portfolio is no longer rebalanced on a monthly basis.

Based on the analysis of the first two FY, I deemed it more efficient not to rebalance this portfolio each month. I'll provide an update on this analysis in a further section of this article.

As you can see, the portfolio has fallen behind the S&P 500 largely due to underperformance in November and December 2024. In Q1 of 2025, the portfolio outperformed the S&P 500 very consistently. But all of those gains were given up in Q2, where the portfolio trailed the S&P 500 each month. The long-term outperformance relative to SCHD remains very high, at 9.08% on an annualized basis. It's worth noting that SCHD has trimmed its loss margin during 2024 but has trailed my portfolio in 2025.

Year-to-date, the portfolio is maintaining a nice lead over SCHD but trails the S&P 500 following the month of June.

What I would like to see from this strategy is a strong total return (12% CAGR) over a long period of time, say 5 to 10 years. So far, so good, but we still have a long road ahead of us.

Dividend Review

Currently, the portfolio has a forward dividend yield of 2.18%, which has decreased from the 2.28% dividend yield a month ago. This is largely due to positive capital appreciation last month, driving the market value of the portfolio higher. The portfolio generated $4.85 in dividend income during the month of June; the dividends were reinvested in a way to reduce the allocation drift. June 2025 dividend income was 21.86% higher than June 2024 dividend income. FY3 is certainly seeing much more variation in the monthly dividend income relative to prior fiscal years, but it is generating dividend growth relative to both prior fiscal years.

Here is a breakdown of dividend income by fiscal year.

FY1 = $41.29

FY2 = $54.60 (+32.24% increase from FY1)

FY3 = $40.42 (8 months into FY3)

Comparing the first 8 months of FY2 to FY3, we can measure a 5.67% rate of dividend growth.

Another way to measure dividend growth is by comparing the projected annual dividend income, or PADI. At the portfolio launch on November 1, 2022, the PADI was $46.79. Clearly, the portfolio did not generate the expected dividend income in FY1, which was primarily due to missing some dividend income in the first month and the timing of monthly rebalancing. Currently, the PADI is $59.68. Comparing the PADI at portfolio launch versus today points to a 27.55% dividend growth. If we annualize this over the 32 months of this portfolio's existence, it equates to a 9.55% dividend CAGR.

In addition to tracking the dividend income and growth of this portfolio, I am also tracking how it compares to the dividend income and growth generated by SCHD over the same time period.

In FY1, an equivalent investment into SCHD, assuming all dividends were reinvested, would have generated $64.63 in dividend income. My portfolio came up 36.11% short of SCHD in terms of dividend income generated.

In FY2, the SCHD equivalent portfolio would have generated $75.09 in dividend income. My portfolio came up 27.29% short of SCHD, while still trailing, this can be seen as an overall improvement.

For perspective, SCHD's dividend growth rate in terms of real dividends paid, between FY1 and FY2, was 16.18% (versus 32.24% for my portfolio).

Given the higher forward dividend yield in my portfolio for FY3, I can hypothesize that the gap in dividend income relative to SCHD will be further trimmed in the upcoming fiscal year.

Thus far in FY3, SCHD is generating 33.75% more dividend income than my portfolio. However, following SCHD's most recent dividend payment, my portfolio is lagging one month of dividend income for an apples-to-apples comparison. If July's dividends are in-line with dividends received in April, SCHD's lead will shrink to about 23%.

4-Factor List For July 2025

Since June 2023, I have been running the 4-factor stock screener on a monthly basis and tracking the list of top-ranked stocks. I want to accumulate this data to run additional tests on more frequent rebalancing and to document how much the list changes from month to month.

Compiling the list is a 2-step process; the first part is generating a shortlist of high-quality dividend growth stocks; the second step is ranking them based on the 4 factors.

I ran this screener on June 30th, and 122 unique dividend growth stocks were selected for further analysis. I then applied the 4-factor stock selection process and narrowed the list down to just the top 20 ideas. The list is presented below, with data shown as of June 30, 2025.

Introduction

Altria Group (NYSE: NYSE:MO) is a major tobacco company that owns brands such as Marlboro, that dominates the U.S. Market. Altria solely focuses on the American market as it spun off Philip Morris International to focus on the rest of the world. The move had the goal to rebrand the company in order to distance itself from the negative image of tobacco and spinning off PMI to avoid U.S.-based litigations and constraints.

Altria has certainly been on the radar for many income-focused investors as it largely attracts shareholders for its yield. I believe that the company still offers and that it can still thrive in a declining smoking environment.

Operation Overview

Altria has two key business segments that reflect its legacy operations, coupled with diversifying efforts.

Smokeable products fall under the umbrella of Philip Morris USA, that focuses on legacy historical cigarette brands such as Marlboro and Virginia Slims but also cigar brands like Black & Mild. Marlboro holds close to 46% of the cigarette market, which easily gives it the dominant position in the U.S. Black & Mild is also a dominant presence within the machine-made cigar sector. The segment generated $20.92B for the LTM, which has been seeing a steady decline since 2020 from $23B generated then.

The Oral Tobacco Products segment consists of moist smokeless tobacco and the popular incoming segment, oral nicotine pouches with the brand on!. Looking at market share, Copenhagen has the dominant position in the U.S.'s moist smokeless tobacco with around 50%, while on! Pouches are growing fast and competing with PMI’s Zyn. The segment generated $2.78B in sales for the LTM.

Current Dynamics

To be clear, cigarette volumes in the U.S. will decline and have already declined faster than the global market’s average. Furthermore, Altria has made some poor choices in diversifying efforts, especially with the $12.8B investment in Juul Labs, which has been swapped for Heated Tobacco Intellectual Property Rights. Altria also lost the rights to market IQOS heated tobacco in the U.S. which could have had the highest potential among emerging tobacco products.

For all the negatives, however, I believe that there are still benefits to be in the U.S. market that can pass on higher prices to offset lower overall volumes. This is due to cigarettes still being quite affordable in the country compared to developed countries and also among developing nations as well. This could certainly leave room for future price increases. Generally speaking, if product price increases, demand for the product decreases, though that’s not the case for cigarettes due to the addictive nature of the product. This keeps customers coming back with a high failure rate of quitting attempts. Furthermore, smokers have shown extreme brand loyalty, which would help Altria’s pricing power and that's why Marlboro has kept such a strong hold on the market, as smokers rarely switch from one brand to another.

Outside of sheer loyalty, Altria’s moat is also helped by the fact that the market is almost impossible to enter for new entrants. Advertising is heavily restricted in the US which heavily stops smokers from even knowing new brands. And let’s say that even if new entrants start to take market share from Altria, this would trigger a rebate amount from the Master Settlement Agreement payments based on share levels of 1997, which would lower future payments.

Valuation

Altria is on the cheaper side of the tobacco industry, as highlighted by its A grade from Seeking Alpha. Altria has a P/E Non-GAAP of 11.16, around 35% lower than the sector median, while also boasting a 9.64 EV/EBTDA, which is around 21% lower than the sector median as well. Altria also has made a tremendous job at boosting its FCF yield over the years, which currently stands at 8.6%. Compared to Philip Morris International that has seen its stock price appreciate tremendously due to the fast growth of Zyn, Altria still remains at bargain levels despite the 26% rally in MO shares.

Overall, revenue growth has been stagnant, with a 1.29% CAGR drop over the past 3 years. Again, that’s mainly from the lack of exposure to international markets and nicotine pouches not offsetting smokable products. Though one thing I would like to note is the incredible rise in net income, showing us the pricing power of the company and the oral segment growing with higher overall margins. Thanks to this pricing power, Altria has been able to grow its gross margin from 58.4% to 70.8%. I believe that growth will remain somewhat stagnant or slightly positive for the company in the future.

Altria also has top-notch profitability metrics with an A+ grade from Seeking Alpha. That’s in part thanks to its incredible 35.5% ROTC, one of the largest I have ever seen that allows Altria to vastly exceed its cost of capital, offering a strong buffer from even the largest hypothetical volume decline. Net margin is also off the chart for Altria at around 50.4% for the LTM, thanks to the vast supply chain and production efficiencies that are now unparalleled in the industry.

This profitability has helped to finance Altria’s high dividend yield, currently standing at 7.02%, a rare sight for a non-MLP company. The dividend has grown by a strong 6.97% CAGR over the past 10 years. This is also accompanied by 56 years of consecutive increases, making Altria part of the dividend aristocrats. Altria also has financed a strong share repurchase program with total shares outstanding being reduced from 1,957.9MM to 1,684.5MM, with another $1B program to be completed during the year. In all, that’s a total of 13.97% reduction.

Risks

Altria operates in the tobacco industry, which in today’s day and age is vowed to decline, with countries such as New Zealand even introducing bans on cigarettes. In the U.S., the Biden administration started to introduce bans on Menthol cigarettes, which could have increased the risk of certain smokers stopping to smoke, though the Trump administration has rolled back some of these measures. Lawsuits are always a risk for these companies, although the MSA in the US has led to more predictable litigation costs.

The decline in cigarette volume is heavily factored in the stock, but Altria has done a poor job at the moment to diversify away from traditional cigarette operations with poor investments, highlighted by Juul Labs. As investors, we have to wait and see if the efforts in nicotine pouches will be enough to offset Zyn’s dominant market share and sustain growth for the company.

Conclusion

With its 7% dividend yield and its incredible moat in the cigarette business, I believe that Altria is a buy despite the structural challenges that the company faces. It still is a cash-generating machine that will be able to grow its dividend and share buyback program despite the possibility of smaller revenue numbers, thanks to its ever-increasing margins. I rate Altria as a buy at current prices.

data.title: Palantir: The Stock Is Overhyped And Overpriced
data.description:
Introduction

Palantir Technologies (NASDAQ:PLTR) creates big data analytics software platforms that assist governments and companies in making difficult decisions. Its products are, Palantir Gotham and Foundry, allow for the connecting and analyzing of sensitive information for security agencies and commercial clients in sectors like finance and pharmaceuticals. Gotham is used chiefly by intelligence agencies and the military to fight terrorism, and Foundry helps companies with data processing. The company is riding the AI boom.

Fundamentals

Palantir Technologies is overvalued now, particularly if you consider the price-to-sales (P/S) ratio. The stock's price to sales ratio is 110.30 for Palantir. As a reference, a P/S between 10 and 20 is what you have for high-growth stocks.

=> 100 is quite uncommon and suggests overvaluation.

The stock rose from $12.45 in 2024 to $140 by June 2025. This rise in this P/S ratio (FWD) from 13 to 79.20 proves that the stock price has risen significantly more than the P/S ratio and is therefore overvalued with the current growth figures. Even after all this growth, Palantir would have to exponentially increase its sales so that it could sustain such a valuation.

Reported operating cash flow: $1,335 million.

- less share-based payments (SBC): $722 million.

- less interest income: $203 million.

= Actual operating cash flow: $410 million.

This shows that the FCF of Palantir is only a fraction ~1/3 of the operating cash flow due to the high share-based payments.

In addition, since 2019 revenue increased by 280% but per share by -1.5%. This shows how the sales performance is relativized by the dilution of the shares, which in my opinion is extremely unattractive.

2019: $1.29 per share.

2024: $1.27 per share.

Now you could say that the biggest dilution was in 2019, so let's look at the year 2023 to 2024, here we have a sales growth of 28.7% which, however, only grows by 22% due to the dilution.

Overall, Palantir is grossly overvalued with its very high P/S ratio, share dilution, and low cash flow. Unless the company performs to the high expectations, a correction is near.

Peer Groups

To value Palantir Technologies Inc. based on the P/S multiple of a peer, I prefer C3.ai Inc. as a better peer compared to Snowflake because it engages in AI-powered analytics for government and commercial customers, similar to Palantir's Gotham and Foundry software platforms. Both firms capitalize on big data and AI, with C3.ai targeting defense and enterprise verticals (e.g., U.S. Air Force, Shell) similar to Palantir's mix of government (61% of sales) and commercial clients in military, finance and pharma. In contrast to Snowflake's commercial cloud data warehousing pure play, C3.ai's enterprise AI and government business more directly match Palantir's business model, so a more suitable basis for comparison. P/S is the preferred ratio to value high-growth technology firms such as Palantir and C3.ai, which are focused on growing revenues at the expense of near-term profits. C3.ai's P/S (FWD) ratio is around 7.03x based on current market prices, versus 79.20x of Palantir. Palantir's trailing 12-month revenues are $3.115 billion, with 33% growth (yoy). In addition, the entire sector has a P/S (FWD) ratio of 3.03, which confirms that Palantir is currently overvalued and needs a lot of growth for the share to be fairly valued in the long term.

Using the P/S ratio of C3.ai, the implied market capitalization of Palantir is $3.115 billion × 7.03 = $21.89 billion. Yet, Palantir's current market capitalization stands at $340.42 billion, a massive premium. This mismatch mirrors Palantir's lucrative exclusive government contracts (e.g., CIA, DoD), AI leadership through its AIP platform, and 340% stock run in 2024 fueled by market speculation. So, using C3.ai's 7.03x P/S multiple, Palantir would be worth $21.89 billion, implying overvaluation compared to its peer. This raises the question of whether Palantir's first mover advantage and leadership + government relations are worth the premium. I currently say no.

Technicals

The chart of Palantir currently confirms my thesis of a correction, as we could currently be in the A-B-C correction wave. This could turn into a correction of 33% to [c] up to 65%. I rather see the Fibonacci of 0.382 coming here. My thesis is also confirmed by the fact that shortly before the end of my analysis, Palantir fell by 7%, thus initiating the potential correction.

Bullish Risks

My bearish thesis could be beaten by.

1: Palantir in the hype, the P/S ratio continues to rise as in the last few years and thus also the chart.

2: Palantir has stronger growth in the future and thus the valuation is relativized, e.g. by the increase in NATO spending to 5%, which is now also going in the direction of AI or new customers are acquired.

Conclusion

In my opinion, Palantir is an overvalued company at the status quo which has a too high P/S ratio and a very low growth due to the dilution, for these reasons, as well as the possible chart correction, my rating is Sell for Palantir.

data.title: SiriusPoint And Its Preferred Stock Are Underrated Opportunities In The Insurance Sector
data.description:
Co-authored by Strategic Yield.

In this article, we want to draw the attention of investors seeking an attractive opportunity: SiriusPoint Ltd.(NYSE:SPNT) and its preferred stock, the cumulative 8% (NYSE:SPNT.PR.B).

Company Overview

SiriusPoint is a global insurer and reinsurer, headquartered in Bermuda and with offices in New York, London, Stockholm, and elsewhere. The company was formed in 2021 from Sirius Group (founded in 1945) and Third Point Re. The total assets at the end of the first quarter of 2025 are close to $12.3 billion, and the current market capitalization is $2.38 billion. Below, you can see a 5-year comparison between SPNT and S&P 500:

SiriusPoint has outperformed the S&P 500 index in total return over the past five years, a testament to its effective management and strong market position. We can dig a little deeper by comparing SPNT's total return over the past five years to that of other highly regarded companies in the industry:

Credit Score

The company stands in the investment grade sector with its credit scores from the major credit companies. Moody's rates the financial strength of the company with an A3, Fitch with A-, S&P with A-, and AM BEST with A-, as you can see below:

These ratings confirm SiriusPoint's strong financial position and strength.

The Preferred Stock

SPNT has one preferred stock:

SPNT.PR.B is trading above par at the moment, at a price of $25.26 with a current yield of 7.89%. The share has a resettable rate after the first call date and does not have a fixed maturity, making it suitable for investors seeking long-term income with growth potential as interest rates rise. Although it does not have an official credit rating from the major agencies, its high current yield makes it competitive with other similar issues. The preferred stock is not rated by S&P, Fitch, or Moody's. It has no specific maturity date. Its call date is on 02/26/2026 (with 0.66 years to call) with a 7.01% yield to call.

OTC Bond

SPNT has one over-the-counter bond with a 7% coupon rate and a maturity date of 04/05/2029:

It has a Baa2 rating from Moody's, which places it at the lower end of investment grade. And the range of its yield is between 5.3-5.4%.

Comparison To Peers

Below are the listed preferred stocks priced above par that are trading before their call dates and are evaluated by their yield to the worst metrics.

We can see that SPNT.PR.B outperforms with a higher yield, even stocks that are rated as investment grade by the credit agencies.

Conclusion

SiriusPoint demonstrates a solid financial position, investment-grade credit rating, and impressive returns relative to the market and peers. SPNT.PR.B offers attractive yields and growth potential, making it an attractive opportunity for investors seeking long-term income with moderate risk.

data.title: PSQ: Hedging Tool In Bear Markets
data.description:
Introduction

In today's article, we will analyze the exchange-traded fund called ProShares Short QQQ ETF (NYSEARCA:PSQ). This fund is designed to offer inverse returns to the Nasdaq 100 on a daily basis, i.e., its objective is to replicate the performance of the index multiplied by -1. The owner and manager of the fund, ProShares, applies a mathematical model to achieve the aforementioned objective.

What this model does is decide on the combination of swaps, futures, and cash that the fund should have in its portfolio in order to achieve the target inverse return. This process is updated daily at the close of each session to calculate the fund's daily NAV. Its costs, as indicated by the Total Expense Ratio, are 0.95% per annum, which is considered balanced within the vehicles in its category.

The main objective of this vehicle is to offer short-term coverage against technology stocks. Under no circumstances should it be considered as a medium- or long-term investment, as investors would lose most of their capital. This reflection can be seen in the image below. The medium- to long-term return on the vehicle is negative across the board. Only in periods such as 2022–2023, with the tightening of monetary policy, or at the beginning of this year, with the introduction of the tariff war by the Trump administration, did the fund appreciate briefly in the short term.

Analysis – Macro Fundamental

In this second section, we will analyze the current conditions and possible catalysts that lead us to consider the option of entering the analyzed vehicle, in a short-term operation as a hedge against technology. To do this, we will analyze different macroeconomic metrics, trying to offer a current and future perspective on each one.

The first indicator selected is known as the Buffett Indicator, with the corresponding study of its historical deviations. As we can see in the image, the indicator is currently at its highest level. These levels have historically preceded a sharp correction, caused by a series of catalysts that we are unable to observe at this time. If there were a short-term decline in aggregate valuations of, say, 10%, we could see corrections in the Nasdaq 100 of the same magnitude, generating positive returns for the vehicle analyzed. Although at the moment I cannot foresee what could cause such a decline in aggregate valuations, it is advisable to closely monitor this type of indicator.

The following chart compares the strength of the dollar against an international basket of currencies and the yield on 10-year US Treasury bonds. This metric is important because companies listed on the Nasdaq 100 index trade at high multiples due to the discounted cash flow valuations they perform. This discounting of cash flows is done by discounting these future flows at the WACC (Weighted Average Cost Of Capital) rate, which is composed of the cost of financing equity and the cost of financing debt. The cost of financing debt is directly related to the IRR of the 10-year bond, as it is considered the risk-free rate. Generally, when there is an increase in this IRR, valuations decrease because the discount denominator increases. Also, when the IRR of this type of bond rises above 4%, it makes these types of products more attractive, and there is a rotation of capital from equities to fixed income and term deposits. If all this occurs, there is an increase in the profitability of the PSQ in the short term.

On the other hand, when the DXY is strong, there are capital withdrawals from emerging markets and carry assets. This causes some of those capital flows that were outside the US to return, but not to growth stocks, but to fixed income products (bonds) and liquidity (deposits).

The following image shows a comparison between Bitcoin and the spread between the 10-year Treasury bond and the 2-year Treasury note. As readers will know, Bitcoin currently acts as a proxy for global sentiment and as a liquidity indicator. When it falls sharply, it is usually accompanied by declines in the technology markets (Nasdaq 100). If we study Bitcoin as an indicator of this type, we can anticipate declines in the technology markets and position ourselves in vehicles such as the PSQ ETF to obtain extra returns on our investments. As for the spread, it is important to study the moments when the curve reverses (spread < 0) as this anticipates recessions and usually coincides with periods when private investment declines and private borrowing costs increase. If you see Bitcoin falling to relative lows and the credit spread approaching or falling below zero, it is advisable to rotate your portfolio to instruments with the PSQ to hedge against a general market decline.

The UM Consumer Sentiment Index is an indicator that measures short-term consumer confidence in the economy. When there is an increase in the indicator, it means that consumers are more confident and therefore more willing to invest. As we can see in the graph, there is a slight divergence between the indicator and the SP500, with the former declining and the latter reaching historic highs. This divergence usually indicates that the current rises in the equity markets are based more on institutional flows and leverage than on the perception of real economic health. For a potential investor in the PSQ ETF, this divergence could be considered a warning sign, as historically, episodes where the indicator does not exceed 70 coincide with phases of consolidation or correction in the indices, providing windows of opportunity to capture returns inverse to the Nasdaq-100.

Finally, we analyze the 1-Month Ahead Macroeconomic Uncertainty indicator. This indicator acts as an early warning system for event-driven risks. When this index rises alongside volatility indicators such as the VIX, the possibility of a technical pullback in the Nasdaq-100 increases, offering an ideal scenario for increasing positions in the PSQ vehicle before the market corrects.

Conclusion

In conclusion, and to wrap up this article, I currently cannot see any potential catalysts that would prompt us to take an immediate position in this vehicle. What I do recommend is monitoring what has been analyzed in this article to see how the different variables studied evolve and, as they move, consider whether we can anticipate a fall in the Nasdaq-100 and position ourselves in the vehicle analyzed accordingly. At the moment, I do NOT recommend buying, but we must remain vigilant. Thank you for reading.
